Evidence: payment-api error_rate jumped from ~0.1% to ~92% at 10:25:00Z and the metric notes correlation with deploy v2.3.1; dependency map shows payment-api depends on postgres-primary so DB connection failures explain widespread 500s.","affected_service":"payment-api","recommendation":"Immediate actions (ordered): 1) Roll back payment-api to last known good release (v2.3.0): kubectl -n <ns> rollout undo deployment/payment-api --to-revision=<v2.3.0-revision> or redeploy image payment-api:v2.3.0. 2) If rollback is not possible, hot-fix v2.3.1 by restoring correct DB env vars and connection configuration (ensure DB_HOST, DB_PORT, DB_USER, DB_PASSWORD and DB pool settings match v2.3.0), then restart payment-api pods to reinitialize DB connections. 3) Add/verify readiness probe that attempts a simple DB connection before marking pods ready to avoid serving traffic on DB init failure. 4) Post-mortem and prevention: add canary/deployment-stage smoke tests that validate DB connectivity, add an alert for error_rate >5% within 15m of a deploy, and add structured logging for DB connection errors to speed future triage."}}

parameters.root_cause: Deployment of payment-api v2.3.1 at ~2026-04-01T10:25:00Z introduced a regression in DB connection initialization. The v2.3.1 image changed DB client/connection-string parsing such that the DB_HOST value is missing/malformed (env var handling change), causing payment-api to fail to establish connections to postgres-primary (connection errors/timeouts) and surface as HTTP 500s. Evidence: payment-api error_rate jumped from ~0.1% to ~92% at 10:25:00Z and the metric notes correlation with deploy v2.3.1; dependency map shows payment-api depends on postgres-primary so DB connection failures explain widespread 500s.
parameters.recommendation: Immediate actions (ordered): 1) Roll back payment-api to last known good release (v2.3.0): kubectl -n <ns> rollout undo deployment/payment-api --to-revision=<v2.3.0-revision> or redeploy image payment-api:v2.3.0. 2) If rollback is not possible, hot-fix v2.3.1 by restoring correct DB env vars and connection configuration (ensure DB_HOST, DB_PORT, DB_USER, DB_PASSWORD and DB pool settings match v2.3.0), then restart payment-api pods to reinitialize DB connections. 3) Add/verify readiness probe that attempts a simple DB connection before marking pods ready to avoid serving traffic on DB init failure. 4) Post-mortem and prevention: add canary/deployment-stage smoke tests that validate DB connectivity, add an alert for error_rate >5% within 15m of a deploy, and add structured logging for DB connection errors to speed future triage.

parameters.root_cause: inventory-service (Kubernetes deployment inventory-service, image tag v2.3.1) is the root cause. Starting ~2026-04-01T09:19Z the inventory-service pods began rapidly restarting due to out-of-memory conditions: container OOM kills and runtime OOM errors (observed as process exits and restart cycles correlated with checkout p99 spikes). The service includes an unbounded in-memory cache introduced in v2.3.1, which grows under normal load and causes heap/heap fragmentation and GC thrashing until the container is OOMKilled. Each restart leaves inventory unavailable for short windows, causing synchronous checkout-service requests to block and produce p99 latencies >5s. Dependency impact: checkout-service blocks on inventory-service calls; redis-cache and postgres are healthy and not the proximate cause.
parameters.recommendation: Immediate (within minutes): 1) Roll back inventory-service to the last known-good image v2.2.8 and scale to previous replica count. 2) Temporarily increase container memory limit/requests for inventory-service from 512Mi -> 1Gi to reduce restart frequency during mitigation. 3) Restart inventory-service pods (kubectl rollout undo deployment/inventory-service OR kubectl set image to v2.2.8, then kubectl rollout restart deployment/inventory-service). 4) In parallel, add a circuit-breaker/timeout in checkout-service for inventory calls (client timeout 800ms, fail-fast path) and shorten retry/backoff to prevent cascade. Medium-term (24–72 hours): 5) Patch inventory-service (v2.3.2) to fix memory leak by bounding the in-memory cache (add MAX_CACHE_ENTRIES and LRU eviction) and fix any allocations causing GC pressure. 6) Deploy v2.3.2 to canary, monitor memory and restart rate, then promote. 7) Add k8s liveness/readiness tuning (initialDelaySeconds 60, failureThreshold backoff) and alerting on pod restart rate and container memory usage >80%. 8) Add bench/regression tests for cache growth. Monitoring/verification: confirm checkout p99 drops below SLA within 2–5 minutes after rollback + restart; ensure inventory pod restarts stop and memory usage stabilizes. Notify on-call inventory-service owner to execute rollback and follow-up fixes.

parameters.recommendation:
Immediate mitigation (next 0-15 minutes):
1) Stop/disable the running analytics job on analytics-worker (cancel quarterly_event_analysis) to free DB connections immediately (e.g. stop the analytics-worker job/process or use the orchestration UI to cancel the job).
2) On postgres-primary, identify and terminate long-running analytics connections: run SELECT pid, application_name, state, now()-query_start AS duration, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE application_name='analytics-worker' OR (query LIKE '%quarterly_event_analysis%' OR state='active') ORDER BY duration DESC; then for offending pids run SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) to free slots.
3) After freeing connections, allow auth-service pool to recover; if services remain degraded, restart auth-service pods/instances to clear stalled requests and rewarm pools.
Short-term fixes (hours):
4) Deploy a connection pooler (pgbouncer) in transaction pooling mode in front of postgres-primary to protect against connection storms; configure JDBC pools to use pgbouncer.
5) Temporarily increase postgres max_connections only if necessary and safe for current resources (e.g. +50) and monitor CPU/memory — prefer pooling over increasing limits.
Long-term remediation (days-weeks):
6) Rework analytics-worker to run heavy reads against a read-replica (provision read replica), limit its DB pool size (recommend max connections 10-20 for analytics), set statement_timeout (e.g. 2m) and query batching/ backoff, and ensure it uses a shared connection pool rather than opening many connections concurrently.
7) Add alerting on postgres_active_connections > 70% of max and on long-running queries, and add runbook steps for terminating runaway analytics queries.
These actions will restore auth-service availability quickly (by freeing DB connections) and prevent recurrence by isolating analytics load from primary transactional workloads.
